region_of_origin: Middle East / Central Asia (orange variety cultivated in Netherlands)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw peeled carrot weighs approximately 85g and provides about 35 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Carrot (raw, peeled) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F14040008; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Carrots, raw, NDB 11124)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo Food Code F14040008 is an exact match for raw peeled orange carrot. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy NDB 11124 (Carrots, raw) and found consistent. HPB values used as primary source for Singapore context.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for raw carrots
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of raw carrots reported as approximately 47 (low) in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycaemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (47 × 9.6 × 0.85) / 100 ≈ 3.4 per 85g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (320 mg/100g), moderate sodium (69 mg/100g), rich beta-carotene
hypertension.guidance: Raw carrots provide a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (approximately 4.6:1) that supports blood pressure regulation; the high beta-carotene content (835 mcg RAE/100g) also contributes antioxidant protection to vascular endothelium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~47), low glycaemic load (~3.4 per 85g serving), dietary fibre 2.8 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite their naturally sweet flavour and 9.6 g carbohydrate per 100g, raw carrots have a low GI of approximately 47 and a glycaemic load of only 3.4 per standard serving, making them safe for blood glucose management in people with Type 2 Diabetes; the soluble fibre (pectin) further slows carbohydrate absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.0 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high beta-carotene and dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw carrots contain no saturated fat or cholesterol, and their high beta-carotene (835 mcg RAE/100g) and dietary fibre (2.8 g/100g) content support LDL cholesterol reduction and oxidative stress protection, making them a cardioprotective food suitable for daily consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High beta-carotene antioxidant content (835 mcg RAE/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beta-carotene in raw carrots acts as an antioxidant carotenoid that may reduce oxidative stress in joint tissue; bioavailability is significantly enhanced when carrots are consumed with a small amount of dietary fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The common orange carrot (Daucus carota) is one of the most micronutrient-dense root vegetables widely available in Singapore and globally. The characteristic orange colour derives from beta-carotene, which the body converts to vitamin A — raw peeled carrot provides 835 mcg RAE of vitamin A per 100g, making it one of the richest plant-based dietary vitamin A sources. Despite its naturally sweet flavour and relatively high carbohydrate content (9.6 g/100g), raw carrots have a low glycaemic index of approximately 47 and a very low glycaemic load of approximately 3.4 per standard 85g serving, making them suitable for people managing Type 2 Diabetes. Dietary fibre content is 2.8 g/100g, primarily from pectin and cellulose, which slows carbohydrate digestion and supports gut health. The potassium content (320 mg/100g) combined with moderate sodium (69 mg/100g) provides a favourable ratio for blood pressure management. Beta-carotene bioavailability is significantly enhanced by concurrent fat consumption — cooking carrots in oil or pairing them with avocado or nuts substantially improves absorption. Raw carrots are used across all Singapore cuisine styles: stir-fried in Chinese dishes, added to Malay curries and soups, incorporated into Indian sabzi, eaten raw as a snack, and used as a filling in popiah. Texture is firm and crunchy when raw, becoming tender when cooked. Flavour is mildly sweet with earthy undertones.
common_user_queries: is carrot good for blood pressure

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F14040008), an approved primary regional source, with cross-reference confirmation from USDA SR Legacy NDB 11124. GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Ontology codes verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT. All values are internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for raw carrot not separately reported in HPB Foodo F14040008; USDA SR Legacy NDB 11124 reports total fat as 0.24g with minor MUFA/PUFA fractions below 0.1g each — these are captured within the total fat_g value of 0.2g. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo F14040008 primary record (USDA SR Legacy reports approximately 88.3g water/100g for reference). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy — carrots are a plant food and naturally contain no vitamin B12 or vitamin D. saturated_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace per HPB Foodo F14040008 and USDA SR Legacy; naturally very low in a raw root vegetable. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as carrots are a plant food containing no cholesterol. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero in an unprocessed raw vegetable.
